content: Just as the linguistic definition of reflexion frames it as both a light reflection off a surface and a deliberate cognitive reconsideration, current LLM self-reflection mechanisms can be redesigned to embrace this dual nature: rather than only reflecting on their own past outputs to improve performance, LLMs should also 'reflect off' external, structured linguistic frameworks of reflexive practice built from second language learning curricula to anchor unguided self-criticism. This dual design addresses a key flaw of modern LLM self-reflection: that unconstrained self-revision often leads to drifting from the core problem or hallucinating incorrect corrections.
